questions.text: I hold the view that a life lived for others is the most meaningful kind of life.
I find that engaging with emotional situations analytically, rather than emotionally, consistently leads to more useful and more equitable outcomes.
I believe that the less I focus on my own needs, the more I can focus on what really matters.
I find that I read interpersonal dynamics like a language I have always been fluent in.
A beautiful piece of music can bring me to tears almost instantly.
I prefer fluid, evolving roles in collaborative work where people contribute where they can.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel in response to suffering is the quality that makes my participation in community life meaningful.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral imperative that applies to me regardless of whether I have a personal connection to those affected.
I hold the view that my emotional responses are like a fire that catches fast and burns hot.
I routinely take credit for my contributions because acknowledging my own work matters to me.
I tend to feel emotionally prepared for whatever comes, because my system doesn't overreact.
I make decisions quickly and stand by them.
I believe that preparation is what separates success from failure in most endeavors.
I gravitate toward being the one who decides when a discussion has gone on long enough.
I tend not to notice when someone is performing friendliness rather than genuinely feeling it.
I document lessons learned from past experiences to avoid repeating mistakes.
I tend to let conversations flow where the group takes them.
I detect when someone's agreement comes with unspoken conditions or reservations.
